metadata.title: Instrument Cluster — OTA Over-The-Air Software Update System Requirements Specification
metadata.author: Systems Engineering Team
metadata.asil_classification: ASIL-B (safety partition protection); QM (update delivery and orchestration)
metadata.notes: OTA update function must comply with UNECE WP.29 R156 (Software Update Management System). Cybersecurity requirements are traceable to ISO/SAE 21434 TARA-IC and cybersecurity goals CG-IC-001 through CG-IC-004. Safety partition protection requirements are traceable to ISO 26262 Part 8 and reference SMPU configuration defined in SYS-IC-SAF-009.

requirements.title: OTA update delivery via vehicle-internal interface only
requirements.text: The instrument cluster OTA update function SHALL accept software update packages exclusively through the vehicle-internal OTA update interface. The cluster SHALL receive update packages forwarded by the authorized OTA Gateway ECU via the CAN FD backbone bus (SYS-IC-CAN-002) using the ISO 15765-2 large-data transport or via automotive Ethernet DoIP (ISO 13400-2) if the cluster supports an Ethernet interface. The cluster SHALL NOT implement any direct wireless connectivity for receiving update packages. Any update package received from a source other than the designated OTA Gateway ECU CAN/Ethernet node address (as defined in vehicle communication matrix CAN-DBC-001) SHALL be silently discarded.
requirements.rationale: Restricting update delivery to the vehicle-internal network through an authorized gateway reduces the OTA attack surface. Direct external wireless update reception on the cluster would expose the firmware update path to over-the-air attackers without the gateway's security filtering layer. Limiting accepted source addresses to the designated gateway node prevents message injection from other vehicle bus nodes (TARA-IC threat: unauthorized firmware delivery via spoofed OTA message source; cybersecurity goal CG-IC-004).

requirements.title: Update package cryptographic signature verification
requirements.text: The instrument cluster OTA update function SHALL verify the cryptographic digital signature of every received update package before any data is written to flash. Signature verification SHALL use ECDSA with curve P-256 and SHA-256 (ECDSA-P256-SHA256). The OEM code-signing public key used for verification SHALL be provisioned into the cluster secure element during manufacturing and SHALL NOT be modifiable via OTA or diagnostic services. Signature verification SHALL be performed over the entire package payload including all firmware images, calibration data, and the signed package manifest. A package with an absent, invalid, or expired signature SHALL be unconditionally rejected; the rejection SHALL be logged to NVM with the package header metadata and reported via UDS DTC. No partial package data SHALL be written to any flash partition prior to successful signature verification.
requirements.rationale: Code signing is the primary technical control against unauthorized firmware installation. Without signature verification, an attacker with access to the vehicle CAN FD bus (via OBD-II port or compromised gateway) could install arbitrary firmware containing malicious logic or deliberate safety mechanism bypasses (TARA-IC threat: unauthorized firmware replacement; cybersecurity goal CG-IC-001). Storing the public key in a secure element prevents key extraction via debug interface attacks. The no-partial-write rule prevents a time-of-check/time-of-use attack where a partially written package could be activated before full signature verification.

requirements.title: Anti-rollback version enforcement
requirements.text: The instrument cluster OTA update function SHALL enforce a monotonic software version counter stored in the secure element (or OTP-protected memory region) to prevent installation of firmware versions older than or equal to the currently active version. Before accepting an update package for installation, the cluster SHALL compare the firmware version number declared in the signed package manifest against the stored minimum acceptable version. A package declaring a version number lower than the stored minimum acceptable version SHALL be rejected and the rejection SHALL be logged to NVM. On successful installation and post-update validation, the stored minimum acceptable version counter SHALL be incremented to the newly installed version value. The minimum acceptable version counter SHALL be a one-way ratchet — it SHALL NOT be decremented by any diagnostic, OTA, or engineering interface.
requirements.rationale: Firmware downgrade attacks exploit the fact that older firmware versions may contain known, patched vulnerabilities. An attacker who has obtained a valid signature for an older firmware version (e.g., leaked from a previous production batch) could install it to re-expose a known CVE. The monotonic version counter stored in write-once memory ensures that even a validly signed older version cannot be installed once the device has been updated past it (TARA-IC threat: signed firmware downgrade to re-expose vulnerability; cybersecurity goal CG-IC-001). One-way ratchet prevents a compromised engineering session from resetting the counter.

requirements.title: Update installation precondition enforcement
requirements.text: The instrument cluster OTA update function SHALL evaluate and enforce the following preconditions before initiating flash programming of a staged and verified update package: (1) vehicle speed is 0 km/h for a minimum continuous duration of 10 seconds (received via SYS-IC-CAN-003/004); (2) supply voltage is within the range 11.5 V to 15.5 V as specified in SYS-IC-PWR-010; (3) KL15 is in RUN or ACC state; (4) driver has provided explicit consent via the HMI workflow defined in SYS-IC-OTA-011; (5) no active safety-relevant DTC is present that prevents normal cluster operation (queried from the DTC mirror). If any precondition is not met at the time of installation initiation, the installation SHALL be deferred and the deferral reason SHALL be logged. If supply voltage drops below 11.0 V during an active flash write operation, the write SHALL be immediately aborted and the cluster SHALL NOT activate the updated partition; automatic rollback SHALL be triggered per SYS-IC-OTA-007.
requirements.rationale: OTA installation during vehicle motion creates a risk of cluster restart mid-drive, removing all instrument display functions while the vehicle is in motion. Power interruption during flash write is the most common cause of corrupted firmware images — a partially written flash partition that is inadvertently activated produces unpredictable system behavior. The active DTC check prevents OTA from being used to mask a pre-existing safety fault by replacing the firmware that detected it. References: SYS-IC-PWR-010 (voltage preconditions), SYS-IC-CAN-003/004 (vehicle speed signal), UNECE R156 Clause 7.3.3 (precondition requirements for software update execution).

requirements.title: Dual-bank A/B partition scheme with atomic bank switchover
requirements.text: The instrument cluster flash layout SHALL implement a dual-bank (A/B) partition scheme for all updatable firmware images, such that the active firmware (Bank A) and the staged update image (Bank B) occupy separate, non-overlapping flash regions. The OTA update function SHALL write all update image data exclusively to the inactive bank while the active bank remains unchanged and in execution. After the update image has been fully written and write-back verified per SYS-IC-OTA-006, the bank switchover SHALL be performed as an atomic operation using a boot configuration flag stored in a dedicated protected NVM region. The bank switchover flag SHALL be committed only after all flash writes to the inactive bank have completed and been verified. The cluster SHALL execute the new firmware from Bank B only after a hardware reset following the atomic flag commit. If the flag commit itself is interrupted by a power loss, the cluster SHALL boot from Bank A (the previously active bank) on the next power-on.
requirements.rationale: A single-partition update scheme requires overwriting the active running firmware, creating a window during which no valid complete firmware image exists — a power interruption during this window produces a permanently bricked device. Dual-bank A/B partitioning eliminates this window: the active firmware is never modified during the update process, and the switchover is atomic (one flag write). Boot-from-A-on-flag-commit-failure ensures that a power loss during the commit itself does not leave the device in an ambiguous state. This requirement is a foundational safety property for OTA reliability, referenced by UNECE R156 Annex 1 (update robustness requirements).

requirements.title: Post-write flash read-back verification
requirements.text: The instrument cluster OTA update function SHALL verify the integrity of every firmware image written to the inactive flash partition by performing a full read-back SHA-256 hash computation over the written region after the write operation completes and comparing it against the expected hash value declared in the signed package manifest. The read-back verification SHALL be performed before the bank switchover flag is committed per SYS-IC-OTA-005. If the read-back hash does not match the expected value, the inactive partition SHALL be marked as invalid in NVM, the bank switchover SHALL NOT be performed, and the failure SHALL be logged with the computed vs. expected hash values. The cluster SHALL report the write verification failure to the OTA Gateway ECU via the campaign status response.
requirements.rationale: Flash write operations can fail silently due to marginal cell programming, transient supply disturbances, or ECC soft errors in the written data. Without read-back verification, a silently mis-programmed inactive partition would be activated and execute corrupted code that could produce arbitrary behavior. Read-back verification closes the gap between a successful write API return code and actual verified bit-accurate flash content. Computing the hash over the full image rather than per-chunk prevents a scenario where per-chunk verification passes but the combined image is invalid due to metadata inconsistency.

requirements.title: Automatic rollback on failed post-update validation
requirements.text: The instrument cluster OTA update function SHALL implement automatic rollback to the previously active firmware partition if post-update validation fails. Automatic rollback SHALL be triggered by any of the following events on the first boot cycle after a bank switchover: (1) startup BIST failure (SYS-IC-SAF-002); (2) post-update application self-test failure (SYS-IC-OTA-010); (3) failure to reach the application ready state within 5000 ms of KL15 detection. Rollback SHALL be implemented by resetting the bank switchover flag in NVM to point to the previously active bank and executing a hardware reset. After rollback, the cluster SHALL boot the previous firmware from Bank A, log the rollback event and failure cause to NVM, and report the rollback to the OTA Gateway ECU via the campaign status response. The rollback attempt counter SHALL be limited to three per update campaign; if rollback fails three times, the cluster SHALL enter a permanent fault state requiring workshop recovery via UDS.
requirements.rationale: A firmware update that passes pre-installation verification but produces a non-functional or partially functional cluster after installation must not leave the vehicle permanently without an instrument cluster. Automatic rollback restores the known-good previous firmware without requiring workshop intervention. The 5000 ms ready-state timeout bounds the rollback decision window and prevents an infinite boot loop on a firmware that crashes after partial initialization. The three-attempt limit prevents a defective update from causing repeated rollback cycles that could consume flash write endurance. Reference: UNECE R156 Clause 7.3.5 (fallback requirements after failed update).

requirements.title: Secure boot verification on every power-on
requirements.text: The instrument cluster SHALL perform secure boot verification on every power-on cycle, independent of whether an OTA update has occurred. During the ROM boot sequence, before transferring execution to the application firmware, the bootloader SHALL compute the SHA-256 hash of the active firmware partition and verify the result against the ECDSA-P256-SHA256 signature stored in the partition header, using the OEM code-signing public key from the secure element (per SYS-IC-OTA-002). If secure boot verification fails, the bootloader SHALL NOT transfer execution to the application firmware; instead it SHALL: (1) display a static secure boot failure indicator via a minimal bootloader-controlled display output; (2) accept only UDS diagnostic communication for recovery; (3) log the failure to a dedicated secure boot failure counter in OTP-protected memory. The secure boot sequence SHALL complete within 500 ms to remain within the power-on display ready time budget defined in SYS-IC-PWR-005.
requirements.rationale: OTA signature verification (SYS-IC-OTA-002) validates the package at installation time. Secure boot provides a complementary runtime integrity check that detects post-installation tampering of the flash content (e.g., a physical flash memory attack, or a memory fault that corrupts the firmware after installation). Secure boot on every power-on ensures that any compromise of the stored firmware — whether introduced by an attacker or caused by hardware degradation — is detected before the application executes (TARA-IC threat: post-installation firmware tampering via physical flash access; cybersecurity goal CG-IC-001). OTP-based failure counter prevents an attacker from erasing failure evidence.

requirements.title: ASIL-B safety partition write protection during OTA
requirements.text: The instrument cluster OTA update function SHALL ensure that ASIL-B classified software partitions (safety-relevant application code, AUTOSAR BSW safety modules, TRAVEO T2G safety firmware, and ASIL-B calibration data) are protected from modification by the OTA update process during normal OTA campaigns. The TRAVEO T2G SMPU (SYS-IC-SAF-009) SHALL be configured such that the OTA update task's Protection Context does not have write permission to the safety partition flash address range. Any OTA campaign that includes an update to safety-classified partitions SHALL require a separate authorization step: a dedicated safety-update authorization code signed by a secondary OEM safety key (independent from the general OTA signing key) SHALL be verified before the SMPU protection is temporarily relaxed to permit safety partition programming. Safety partition programming SHALL only occur in a dedicated safety update mode entered from a controlled diagnostic session, and SHALL be followed by a mandatory full startup BIST execution before returning to normal operating mode.
requirements.rationale: ISO 26262 Part 8 requires that safety-classified software is protected against modification by non-safety-qualified processes. The standard OTA update process is a QM process; allowing it unrestricted write access to ASIL-B partitions violates Freedom from Interference (FFI) between the QM OTA function and the ASIL-B safety firmware. A compromised OTA process (or a successful OTA attack) that can overwrite ASIL-B safety mechanisms (DCLS configuration, WWDT setup, FAULT unit routing) could silently disable the cluster's entire safety architecture. The separate safety key for safety partition updates creates an independent authorization path that a general OTA key compromise cannot unlock (TARA-IC threat: OTA abuse to disable safety mechanisms; cybersecurity goal CG-IC-003). Reference: ISO 26262 Part 6 Clause 7.4.12 (FFI); TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual Section 11 (SMPU).

requirements.title: Post-update application self-test on first boot
requirements.text: On the first boot cycle after a successful bank switchover, the instrument cluster SHALL execute an extended post-update application self-test in addition to the standard startup BIST (SYS-IC-SAF-002). The post-update self-test SHALL include: (1) verification that all safety mechanism initialization routines (DCLS, WWDT, SMPU, FAULT unit) complete without error; (2) a functional check of the display subsystem output using a defined test pattern on all display zones; (3) verification that all ASIL-tagged CAN signal receive buffers initialize to their defined safe default values; (4) a checksum verification of the active ASIL-B calibration data partition against the expected checksum stored in the package manifest. If any post-update self-test check fails, the result SHALL be recorded in NVM with the failing check identifier and automatic rollback SHALL be triggered per SYS-IC-OTA-007. The post-update self-test SHALL complete within 3000 ms to remain within the first-boot startup time budget.
requirements.rationale: Standard startup BIST (SYS-IC-SAF-002) validates hardware integrity but does not validate that the newly installed firmware correctly initializes all safety mechanisms in the new software version. A firmware update that introduces a software defect in safety mechanism initialization (e.g., incorrect SMPU region configuration, wrong WWDT period constant) would pass hardware BIST but produce a safety-degraded runtime state. The post-update application self-test provides a software-level confidence check specific to the updated version. ASIL-A is assigned because this check is a defence-in-depth measure; the primary integrity control is signature verification (SYS-IC-OTA-002).

requirements.title: Driver notification and explicit consent workflow
requirements.text: The instrument cluster OTA update function SHALL implement a driver notification and consent workflow for all OTA update campaigns that require an installation phase involving a cluster reset. The workflow SHALL be: (1) AVAILABLE: when a verified update package is staged and all preconditions except driver consent are met, the cluster SHALL display an update availability notification on the instrument cluster display, including the update description, estimated duration, and restart requirement; (2) CONSENT: the cluster SHALL require an explicit driver acknowledgement via a steering wheel control input before initiating the installation phase; installation SHALL NOT begin automatically without consent; (3) IN PROGRESS: during installation, the cluster SHALL display a progress indicator and SHALL suppress all other display functions; (4) COMPLETE: after successful installation and rollback-free first boot, the cluster SHALL display an update completion confirmation. For background download and staging operations that do not require a cluster reset, no driver consent is required but a silent notification SHALL be logged. Emergency security updates designated as CRITICAL in the package manifest SHALL display a mandatory notification but MAY proceed after a 60-second consent timeout without explicit driver input.
requirements.rationale: UNECE R156 Clause 7.3.2 requires that the vehicle operator is informed of software updates and that the update process does not unexpectedly disrupt vehicle operation. An OTA-triggered cluster reset without driver awareness during a drive cycle removes all instrument display functions unexpectedly, constituting a safety hazard and a regulatory violation. The 60-second CRITICAL update timeout balances timely security patch deployment with driver awareness requirements. Reference: UNECE R156 Annex 1, Clause 3.3 (notification and consent requirements).

requirements.title: UNECE R156 SUMS audit trail and update history log
requirements.text: The instrument cluster OTA update function SHALL maintain a tamper-evident update history log in NVM, recording every OTA update event throughout the vehicle lifetime. Each log entry SHALL include: software version before update, software version after update (or rollback target version on failure), update campaign identifier received from the OTA Gateway, installation outcome (SUCCESS / ROLLBACK / REJECTED), rejection or rollback reason code, vehicle odometer value at time of update, and a UTC timestamp synchronized from the vehicle network. The update history log SHALL be accessible via the UDS service ReadDataByIdentifier (0x22, data identifier 0xF1A0) in all diagnostic sessions. The log SHALL be integrity-protected by a running HMAC-SHA256 chain, where each entry's HMAC includes the hash of the previous entry, preventing undetected deletion or modification of historical entries. The log SHALL retain a minimum of 50 update event records before overwriting the oldest entry.
requirements.rationale: UNECE R156 requires OEMs to maintain records of software updates applied to each vehicle to support type approval obligations and post-market surveillance. The tamper-evident HMAC chain prevents forensic evidence of a malicious update event from being deleted after the fact — an attacker who successfully installs unauthorized firmware and then attempts to erase the log entry would break the HMAC chain, making the tampering detectable (TARA-IC threat: erasure of update evidence to conceal attack; cybersecurity goal CG-IC-004). The 50-entry minimum accommodates vehicles with high OTA update frequency over a 10-year service life. Reference: UNECE R156 Clause 6.1 (software update records); ISO/SAE 21434 WP-08-06 (vulnerability management evidence).
